clean_story:
Leaving without returning. The moment between here and gone.

The word departure comes from the Old French departir—"to divide or separate," like untying a knot that once held two ropes together. It’s not just movement; it’s the exact instant when connection ends.

A departure starts before the body moves. A train’s brakes hiss as pressure releases, metal groaning like a sigh. Airports hum with the unseen labor of fuel pumps pushing 3,000 gallons per minute into a 747’s tanks—each drop a commitment to distance. At sea, a ship’s hull doesn’t just cut water; it drags a wake, a temporary scar in the ocean that erases itself in under ten minutes.

The most precise departures are silent. Astronauts aboard the ISS feel no jolt when their Soyuz capsule undocks; they float free only when the springs push them away at 0.12 meters per second. On Earth, a migrating bar-tailed godwit takes off from Alaska and flies 11,000 kilometers nonstop to New Zealand, its departure a single wingbeat that outlasts storms.

Three related subjects are final goodbyes in nature, the physics of separation, and how time feels when leaving.
